epigraph.text:
Two are better than one, because they have a good 
return for their labor.

content.text:
Addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division are the four 
fundamental operations of arithmetic. Together, they allow us to combine, 
separate, and compare quantities in virtually every mathematical situation.

**Addition** combines two or more numbers into a 
sum. The numbers being added are called **addends**, and the result 
is the **sum**.

content.title: Adding with regrouping
content.solution:
\[
\begin{array}{r}
 ^1\;^1\;^1\\
 2857 \\
+ 4396 \\
\hline
 7253
\end{array}
\]
Adding right to left:

- Ones: $7 + 6 = 13$ (write 3, carry 1)

- Tens: $5 + 9 + 1 = 15$ (write 5, carry 1)

- Hundreds: $8 + 3 + 1 = 12$ (write 2, carry 1)

- Thousands: $2 + 4 + 1 = 7$
The sum is **7,253**.

content.text:
**Subtraction** finds the difference between two 
numbers. The number we subtract from is the **minuend**, the number 
we subtract is the **subtrahend**, and the result is the 
**difference**.

content.title: Subtracting with borrowing
content.solution:
\[
\begin{array}{r}
 4\;9\;12\;12\\
 5032 \\
- 2758 \\
\hline
 2274
\end{array}
\]
Working right to left:

- Ones: Can't do $2 - 8$, so borrow from tens. But tens is 3, so we 
 need to borrow from hundreds, which is 0, so borrow from thousands.

- After borrowing: $12 - 8 = 4$

- Tens: $12 - 5 = 7$ (after borrowing)

- Hundreds: $9 - 7 = 2$ (after borrowing)

- Thousands: $4 - 2 = 2$
The difference is **2,274**.

content.title: Checking Subtraction
content.content:
To check a subtraction problem, add the difference to the subtrahend. 
The result should equal the minuend.
\[
\text{If } 5{,}032 - 2{,}758 = 2{,}274, \text{ then } 2{,}274 + 2{,}758 = 5{,}032 \; \checkmark
\]

content.text:
**Multiplication** is repeated addition. 
Instead of adding $7 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 7$, we write $5 \times 7 = 35$. The 
numbers being multiplied are **factors**, and the result is the 
**product**.

content.title: Multiplying by powers of 10
content.solution:
1. $56 \times 10 = 560$ (append one zero)

2. $56 \times 100 = 5{,}600$ (append two zeros)

3. $56 \times 1{,}000 = 56{,}000$ (append three zeros)
**Pattern**: Multiplying by $10^n$ appends $n$ zeros to the number.

content.text:
**Division** is the inverse of multiplication. It 
separates a quantity into equal groups. The number being divided is the 
**dividend**, the number we divide by is the **divisor**, and 
the result is the **quotient**. If the division is not exact, we 
also have a **remainder**.

content.title: Long division
content.solution:
\[
\begin{array}{r}
67 \text{ R}6\\
23\overline{)1547}\\
\underline{138}\\
167\\
\underline{161}\\
6
\end{array}
\]

- $23$ goes into $154$ six times ($23 \times 6 = 138$)

- Subtract: $154 - 138 = 16$. Bring down 7 to get 167.

- $23$ goes into $167$ seven times ($23 \times 7 = 161$)

- Subtract: $167 - 161 = 6$ (remainder)
The quotient is **67 R6**, or $67$ with remainder $6$.

We can write this as: $1{,}547 = 23 \times 67 + 6$

content.title: Checking Division
content.content:
To check a division problem:
\[
\text{Dividend} = (\text{Divisor} \times \text{Quotient}) + \text{Remainder}
\]
For the example above: $23 \times 67 + 6 = 1{,}541 + 6 = 1{,}547 \; \checkmark$

content.title: Division by Zero
content.content:
Division by zero is undefined. We cannot divide any number by zero. 
Consider: if $8 \div 0 = n$, then $0 \times n = 8$. But any number times 
zero is zero, never 8. So there is no answer, and we say division by 
zero is **undefined**.

content.text:
The operations of arithmetic follow certain fundamental properties. 
Understanding these properties helps us compute more efficiently and 
understand why our methods work.

content.title: Commutative Property
content.content:
Changing the order of the numbers does not change the result (for 
addition and multiplication):
$$
a + b &= b + a && \text{(Addition)} \\
a \times b &= b \times a && \text{(Multiplication)}
$$

content.title: Associative Property
content.content:
Changing the grouping does not change the result (for addition and 
multiplication):
$$
(a + b) + c &= a + (b + c) && \text{(Addition)} \\
(a \times b) \times c &= a \times (b \times c) && \text{(Multiplication)}
$$

content.title: Identity Property
content.content:
Adding zero or multiplying by one leaves a number unchanged:
$$
a + 0 &= a && \text{(Additive Identity)} \\
a \times 1 &= a && \text{(Multiplicative Identity)}
$$

content.title: Zero Property of Multiplication
content.content:
Any number multiplied by zero is zero:
\[
a \times 0 = 0
\]

content.title: Distributive Property
content.content:
Multiplication distributes over addition:
\[
a \times (b + c) = (a \times b) + (a \times c)
\]

content.title: Using properties to compute
content.solution:
Using the commutative and associative properties:
\[
25 \times 17 \times 4 = (25 \times 4) \times 17 = 100 \times 17 = 1{,}700
\]

content.title: Using the distributive property
content.problem: Use the distributive property to find $6 \times 58$.

marginNotes.text:
Those who accepted his message were baptized, 
and about three thousand were added to their number that day.

But everything should be done in 
a fitting and orderly way.

marginNotes.author: Carl Friedrich Gauss
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is the queen of the 
sciences.
